summary: Zed Shaw documents his migration away from WordPress to a custom blog built with Svelte and Sapper. After using WordPress since 2014 while he studied painting, he grew frustrated with its poor media handling and confusing UI. He credits Svelte's reactive model and the Zettlr writing tool as the catalysts for rebuilding his web presence. The post reflects on how maintaining custom blog software keeps programming skills sharp, something he regrets neglecting during his painting years.
key_points: WordPress is adequate for text but terrible for media, organization, and anything beyond basic blogging
Maintaining custom blog software is a valuable way to stay sharp as a programmer and keep up with technology curves
Svelte's 'assignment is reactive' model represents a significant leap in UI development simplicity
Good writing tools matter: Zettlr's note organization and focus mode enable a better writing workflow than browser-based editors
Six years studying painting improved his visual and UI design abilities
money_quotes: Thinking back this was a terrible idea because my programming skills didn't have anywhere to exercise, and a blog is an excellent way to stay sharp.
You get sucked into doing something that's barely capable and there's not much of a reason to change even if it could be better.
I really want everyone to adopt the 'assignment is reactive' model in the near future and feel that Rich is a genius.
It's so simple I'm constantly asking, 'Why didn't I think of that.'
Just the fact that the backspace key can cause all of my work to disappear gives me hives.
I suspect also my 6+ years studying painting have made my visual abilities stronger so I'm able to handle the UI much better than before.
key_insight: Outsourcing your tools to platforms like WordPress trades short-term convenience for long-term skill atrophy and creative limitation.
